name: Paradise California Fire
description: The Paradise California Fire, also known as the Camp Fire, was a devastating wildfire that occurred in November 2018, largely destroying the town of Paradise, California. While officially attributed to faulty equipment owned by Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E) and dry conditions, various conspiracy theories have emerged surrounding the event. These theories range from claims of deliberate arson by government entities or secret societies to clear land for high-speed rail or other development projects, to the use of directed-energy weapons (DEWs) to intentionally ignite the fire. Proponents often cite anomalies in photographs and videos, such as houses seemingly untouched amidst widespread destruction, as evidence of DEW usage. They also point to the timing of the fire coinciding with discussions about land development and infrastructure projects. Mainstream perspectives attribute the fire to a combination of factors: PG&E's negligence in maintaining its equipment, prolonged drought conditions exacerbated by climate change, strong winds, and the town's location in a fire-prone area. Investigations by Cal Fire and other agencies have concluded that the fire was caused by a faulty PG&E transmission line. Critics of the conspiracy theories emphasize the lack of credible evidence supporting claims of arson or DEW involvement and highlight the scientific consensus on the causes of wildfires. They argue that the anomalies cited by proponents can be explained by variations in construction materials, wind patterns, and fire behavior. The spread of these conspiracy theories has been fueled by social media and a general distrust of government and corporate entities. The trauma and devastation caused by the fire have also created a fertile ground for alternative explanations, as people struggle to make sense of the tragedy.
subtitle: Conspiracy theories surrounding the 2018 wildfire.

claims.claim: The Paradise fire was intentionally started using directed-energy weapons (DEWs).
claims.evidence: Selective destruction of properties, melted vehicles, and anecdotal reports of unusual heat.
claims.criticism: Mainstream science attributes these effects to the intense heat and unpredictable behavior of wildfires. Variations in construction materials and wind patterns explain selective destruction.

claims.claim: The fire was a deliberate act by government or corporate entities to clear land for development projects.
claims.evidence: Timing of the fire coinciding with discussions about high-speed rail and other infrastructure projects, alleged land grabs after the fire.
claims.criticism: Official investigations point to PG&E's faulty equipment as the cause. Development plans existed prior to the fire and are not directly linked to its cause.

claims.claim: The official explanation is a cover-up to protect those responsible for the fire.
claims.evidence: Distrust of government and PG&E, perceived inconsistencies in official reports.
claims.criticism: Extensive investigations by multiple agencies support the official explanation. Conspiracy theories lack verifiable evidence and rely on speculation.

keyFigures.name: Michael Janitch (aka Michael J)
keyFigures.role: Promoter of DEW theory and other conspiracies surrounding the fire.

keyFigures.name: Cal Fire Investigators
keyFigures.role: Conducted the official investigation determining the cause of the fire.

timeline.event: The Camp Fire ignites near Pulga, California.
The fire rapidly spreads, destroying most of Paradise, California.
The Camp Fire is fully contained.
Cal Fire releases its investigation report, attributing the fire to PG&E equipment.
Conspiracy theories about the cause of the fire continue to circulate online.

sources.author: Abigail Higgins
sources.work: Paradise Lost: The Untold Story of California's Camp Fire
sources.title: Abigail Higgins

sources.author: Numerous news outlets
sources.work: Coverage of the Camp Fire and its aftermath
